name: Tamim al-Dari
title: Tamim al-Dari (RA): The Palestinian Companion
meta_description: The story of Tamim al-Dari (RA), the Palestinian Christian scholar and merchant who came late to the Prophet Muhammad, lit the first lamps in the mosque, and hid his worship from the eyes of others.
keywords: Tamim al-Dari, story of Tamim al-Dari, Palestinian companion, sahaba stories, religion is sincerity, al-Jassasa, The Firsts, Filastin
lede: He came to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ in the very last year of his life, a Christian merchant from Palestine who had spent years reading the scriptures and looking for the truth. By the time he arrived, he already loved the man he had only heard about.

This is the story of Tamim al-Dari (may Allah be pleased with him): a scholar who recognised the Prophet ﷺ from the books, lit the first lamps in the mosque, and became a worshipper who could not bear to be seen worshipping.

sections.h2: A merchant looking for the truth
paras: Tamim al-Dari was a Palestinian Christian, living in the land of Sham under the Roman Empire. He was not a priest or a preacher. His trade was the road: he led caravans across distant lands, buying and selling, and in the summers he met the Arab merchants who came up from Makkah to trade.

Through those traders he began to hear of a man in Makkah and Madinah who was calling people to one God. He was a literate, well read man, a careful student of his own religion, the kind of person who reads the scripture closely and wants to know what is true.

At first he assumed this was just another of the pagan movements rising out of the idol worship of the region. But the descriptions did not fit. This was a man calling to the way of Ibrahim, affirming the prophets, affirming Jesus (peace be upon him), affirming the Tawrah and the Injil. The more Tamim heard, the more his curiosity grew into a search.

Tamim did what a truth seeker does: he went and asked. He found a rabbi who was deeply learned and put his question plainly. Where are we in the history of the prophets? Should I be a Jew, should I be a Christian, where should I be exerting myself?

The rabbi told him a prophet was coming, one who would bring everything together, and that it made sense he would rise in that region, from the children of Ibrahim. Then Tamim went to a Christian scholar, a priest, who admitted something heavy: that they had lost their connection to the religion Jesus himself had practised, and that the time for such a prophet had indeed come.

Both of them, the rabbi and the priest, pointed in the same direction. A prophet was due to put a scattered, fractured people back together. Everything Tamim was hearing about Makkah was lining up with what the scholars of the book were telling him at home.

There was one more sign, and it came from his own village near Hebron, in the land of Ibrahim himself. Some of the people there used to go to the abandoned valleys and call upon the jinn. Tamim was with them one day when a voice answered: a righteous jinn who had accepted Islam, telling them to stop. Stop seeking refuge in us, it said. Seek refuge in Allah instead. Your Lord has sent a message and a messenger, and the news of the heavens and the earth has completely changed. From the merchants on the road, from the rabbi, from the priest, and now from a voice in the valley, the same word kept reaching him. So Tamim set out, in the year of the delegations, to find him.

sections.h2: The man in chains, and the gathering in the mosque
paras: On the way, something happened that he would carry to Madinah. Tamim had been at sea with thirty men when a storm seized their boat and dragged them, lost, for a month. At sunset they washed up on a small island none of them knew. There a strange, heavily haired beast met them and called itself al-Jassasa, and it sent them toward a monastery where, it said, a man was waiting, eager for their news.

Inside they found the largest man they had ever seen, his hands chained to his neck, irons on his legs. He questioned them about the date palms of Bisan, about the water of Lake Tiberias, about the unlettered Prophet in Makkah: had the Arabs fought him, and had they followed him? When they told him the Arabs had come to follow the Prophet ﷺ, the man said it was in their interest to do so. Then he named himself. He was the Dajjal, held until the day he would be loosed upon the earth, barred only from Makkah and Madinah, where angels would turn him away.

When Tamim reached Madinah and accepted Islam, the Prophet ﷺ gathered the people to the mosque outside the time of prayer. He sat on the pulpit, smiling, and told them not to move from where they sat. He had not called them out of any fear or alarm, he said, but because this Christian man had come and told him a story that agreed with everything he had already taught them about the Dajjal.

The Prophet ﷺ asked, does this not agree with what I told you? And they said: yes, Messenger of Allah, it agrees with all of it. Tamim's testimony was not the proof. It was a mercy, a way for the faith of the companions to be strengthened by a stranger who had come from the edge of the world.

Tamim and his delegation came with gifts. He brought a fine Arabian horse and a good cloak, and, not yet knowing it was forbidden, a bottle of wine. The Prophet ﷺ did not break it over his head. He simply made it known, gently, that this was now prohibited, and turned the moment into one of teaching.

He had also brought five men he held as slaves. When the Prophet ﷺ said he wished to free them, Tamim answered at once: if you want to free them for the sake of Allah, then I free them for the sake of Allah. There was no process to wait for. He let them go.

But his most beautiful gift was light. Palestine is a land rich in olive oil, and Tamim knew it. He was the first to light oil lamps in the mosque, beautifying it with a whole system of lamps where before there had been darkness. The Prophet ﷺ told him: may Allah grant you light on the Day of Judgement. One of the freed men took charge of keeping the lamps lit, and the Prophet ﷺ gave him a new name tied to that same light.

Tamim did not return home to lead his people. He stayed in Madinah and gave himself to the Prophet ﷺ. He asked careful questions, and one of his answers became a line every Muslim knows. He asked what the religion was, and the Prophet ﷺ said: the religion is sincerity. To whom, they asked. To Allah, to His Book, to His Messenger ﷺ, to the leaders of the Muslims, and to the common people among them.

He lived that word. He became the helpful man in the community, the one who settled new converts and taught them how to pray. He drew on his old scholarship of the scriptures to understand the Quran, and he memorised it so quickly and so thoroughly that he became a teacher of it to companions who had been Muslim from the very beginning. His students included Abdullah ibn Abbas and Anas ibn Malik.

The Prophet ﷺ once paid him the highest compliment of character a man can receive: if I had a daughter who was not married, I would have married her to you. A cousin of the Prophet ﷺ heard this and offered his own daughter to Tamim instead.

sections.h2: The worship he would not show
paras: Tamim fell in love with the Quran. While others completed it in seven days, he was known to recite the whole of it in a single night of prayer. A neighbour said he would hear Tamim repeat one verse and weep over it until morning, the verse about whether those who do evil will be treated like those who believe and do good.

When someone once asked him how much he read at night, Tamim grew angry. It seems to me, he said, that you are one of those people who prays at night and then tells everyone in the morning how much he read. I would rather pray three short units of witr and tell no one, than recite the whole Quran in the night and then announce it.

He guarded his worship from every eye. He even kept a special garment that he wore only on the night he hoped held great reward, and the others learned to watch for it: if Tamim thought tonight was the night, then perhaps it was. He had no interest in being measured. What passed between him and his Lord, he kept between him and his Lord.

ayah.text: Do those who commit evil deeds really think that We will deal with them in the same way as those who believe and do righteous deeds, that they will be alike in their living and their dying? How badly they judge!

paras: Tamim did not live long beside the Prophet ﷺ. He had come only in the final year. Yet his rank was known, and after the Prophet ﷺ passed, the leaders of the Muslims trusted him deeply. When a man wished to repent and return to his faith, he was sent to Tamim, the best of the believers, to be taught and settled again.

When he had first accepted Islam, Tamim had asked the Prophet ﷺ for a piece of land in Bethlehem, knowing that Islam would one day reach it. The Prophet ﷺ granted it to him. Years later, when that land came under Islam, the request was honoured, on the condition that it never be sold. To this day his descendants are spread across Palestine, and they have kept that land as a trust.

He left Madinah during the years of strife and went home to the land of Sham. His grave lies today in occupied Palestine, neglected, with settlers on every side of it. It is not the grave that is sacred but the man, and the reminder he leaves: that this land carried the people of the Prophet ﷺ long before it carried sorrow.

lessons_heading: What Tamim al-Dari's life teaches us
lessons_intro: His story is short in years and long in meaning. He arrived late, gave quietly, and hid the best of what he did.

lessons.bold: Seek the truth, then follow it.
lessons.text: He did not wait for the truth to find him. He asked the rabbi, the priest, and anyone who knew, and when the answers lined up, he travelled to act on them.
He could not change the world, so he lit the room he was in. The lamps of the mosque were his, and the Prophet ﷺ prayed that Allah would light his way in return.
He would rather pray a little in secret than a great deal that others could count. He kept his worship between himself and his Lord, and Allah made him known anyway.

lessons.bold: Sincerity is the whole of it.
lessons.text: When he asked what religion was, the answer was one word: sincerity. He spent the rest of his life proving it in small, unseen ways.

faq.a: A Palestinian Christian scholar and merchant who accepted Islam in the final year of the Prophet Muhammad's life. He is remembered as the Palestinian companion, the one who lit the first lamps in the Prophet's mosque, and a devoted reciter of the Quran.

faq.q: Why is Tamim al-Dari called the Palestinian companion?
faq.a: Because he came to the Prophet ﷺ from the land of Palestine, and early scholars who wrote the great biographies of the companions described him this way. His descendants remain in Palestine to this day.

faq.q: What is the story of Tamim al-Dari and the Dajjal?
faq.a: Before he reached Madinah, Tamim and his companions were lost at sea and washed up on an island where they met a chained giant who named himself the Dajjal. His account agreed with what the Prophet ﷺ had already taught about the end of time, and it strengthened the faith of the companions.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Tamim al-Dari?
faq.a: To seek the truth and act on it, to give whatever good is within reach, and above all to hide one's worship from the eyes of others and keep it sincere for Allah alone.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: It is tempting to think the people who matter most are the ones who arrived early and stood in the front. Tamim arrived late, in the last year, and still his name endures. He did not measure his faith against anyone else's, and he would not let anyone measure it against his. He lit lamps, taught the lost, and wept over a single verse where no one could see.

Most of our own worship happens where no one is watching: a prayer no one knows we prayed, a kindness no one will trace back to us. Tamim's life is an invitation to leave it there, unseen and unannounced, and to want nothing from it except that Allah is pleased. Carry that quiet into today, and his story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Tamim al-Dari (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). The Quran translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 45:21). Names and places have been rendered in their standard seerah spelling. Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

By the time he arrived, he already loved him.

He asked a rabbi. He asked a priest. He asked anyone who knew.

Every answer pointed the same way: a prophet had come, in the land of Ibrahim, calling people back to one God.

Even a voice in the valley reached him.

Lost at sea for a month, his boat washed up on an unknown island.

There a chained giant named himself the Dajjal.

His account matched, word for word, what the Prophet ﷺ had already taught.

Palestine is a land rich in olive oil. Tamim knew it.

He was the first to light the lamps of the mosque.

The Prophet ﷺ told him: may Allah grant you light on the Day of Judgement.

He asked: what is the religion?

The Prophet ﷺ answered: the religion is sincerity.

To Allah, to His Book, to His Messenger ﷺ, to the leaders of the Muslims, and to the common people.

He hid the best of what he did.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
Tamim al-Dari (may Allah be pleased with him) came to the Prophet ﷺ in the final year of his life: a Palestinian Christian scholar and merchant who had searched the scriptures, questioned the rabbis and priests, and travelled a long road to reach a man he had only heard about.

He brought gifts, freed the men he held, and lit the first lamps in the mosque. But the deeper you read, the quieter he becomes. He would recite the whole Quran in a single night and tell no one. He wept over one verse until morning, where no eye could see.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

email.subject: The companion who hid his worship
email.alt_subject: He lit the first lamps in the mosque
email.preview: Tamim al-Dari came late, gave quietly, and refused to let anyone count his prayers.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Some companions arrived early and stood in the front rows of history. Tamim al-Dari (may Allah be pleased with him) arrived late, in the very last year of the Prophet's life, and still his name endures.

He was a Palestinian Christian merchant, a careful reader of the scriptures. Through the traders on the road he began to hear of a man in Makkah calling people to one God. So he asked. He went to a rabbi, then to a priest, and both pointed the same way: a prophet was coming who would put a scattered people back together. He set out to find him.

When he reached Madinah and accepted Islam, he came with gifts. A fine horse, a good cloak, and, not yet knowing it was forbidden, a bottle of wine. The Prophet ﷺ did not scold him. He simply made it known, gently, that this was now prohibited, and taught him.

Then Tamim gave the gift he is remembered for. Palestine is rich in olive oil, and he knew it. He lit the first lamps in the mosque, turning its darkness into light. The Prophet ﷺ told him: may Allah grant you light on the Day of Judgement.

But the part that stays with me is quieter. Tamim fell in love with the Quran and would recite the whole of it in a single night. When a man once asked how much he prayed, he grew angry. "I would rather pray three short units of witr and tell no one," he said, "than recite the whole Quran in the night and then announce it in the morning." A neighbour heard him repeat one verse and weep over it until dawn, where no one was meant to see.

There is a lesson in him that is easy to miss. Most of our worship happens where no one is watching. Tamim's life says: leave it there. Keep it unseen, keep it sincere, and want nothing from it except that Allah is pleased.

When he asked what the religion was, the Prophet ﷺ gave him one word: sincerity.

May Allah be pleased with him, and grant us light on the day we will need it most.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: Tamim al-Dari (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Palestinian Christian, a merchant who led caravans across distant lands and read the scriptures closely. Through the Arab traders on the road, he began to hear of a man in Makkah who was calling people to one God.

He was a truth seeker, so he asked. He went to a learned rabbi, and then to a Christian priest, and both told him the same thing: a prophet was coming, from the children of Ibrahim, who would put a scattered people back together. Even a righteous jinn in the valleys near his village told his people to stop calling on the spirits and seek refuge in Allah, because a messenger had come.

On his way to Madinah, Tamim was lost at sea for a month and washed up on an unknown island. There a chained giant questioned him about the world and then named himself the Dajjal, held until the day he would be loosed. When Tamim reached the Prophet ﷺ and accepted Islam, his account matched, word for word, what the Prophet ﷺ had already taught his companions, and their faith was strengthened.

He came with gifts, and freed the men he held as soon as the Prophet ﷺ wished it. But his finest gift was light. Palestine is rich in olive oil, and Tamim was the first to light the lamps of the mosque. The Prophet ﷺ told him: may Allah grant you light on the Day of Judgement.

He stayed in Madinah and gave himself to the Quran. While others finished it in seven days, he would recite the whole of it in a single night. A neighbour heard him repeat one verse and weep over it until morning, where no one was meant to see.

When a man asked how much he prayed at night, Tamim grew angry. "I would rather pray three short units of witr and tell no one," he said, "than recite the whole Quran and then announce it." He hid the best of what he did.

He asked the Prophet ﷺ once what the religion was. The answer was a single word: sincerity. To Allah, to His Book, to His Messenger ﷺ, to the leaders of the Muslims, and to the common people. Tamim spent the rest of his short life proving it in small, unseen ways.
storybook.pullquote: May Allah grant you light on the Day of Judgement.
storybook.reflection: Tamim arrived in the last year, gave quietly, and refused to let anyone count his prayers. Most of our own worship happens where no one can see: a prayer no one knows we prayed, a kindness no one traces back to us. His life is an invitation to leave it there, unseen and sincere, and to want nothing from it but the pleasure of Allah.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
